rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical multicomponent C-H tetrazolation of alkyl arenes and vicinal azidotetrazolation of alkenes using TMSN3 and acetonitrile via radical-cation/carbocation Ritter-type pathway
electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation: for alkyl arenes, the arene is oxidized to a radical cation that loses a proton to give a benzyl radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a benzyl carbocation; for alkenes, anodic oxidation generates an azido radical from N3- (from TMSN3) that adds to the alkene, and the resulting C-radical is then oxidized to a β-azido carbocation
electron_flow: For C-H tetrazolation: anode oxidizes the alkyl arene to a radical cation, which deprotonates to a benzyl radical and is oxidized again at the anode to a benzyl carbocation; MeCN traps the cation to form a nitrilium ion, which is attacked by azide to deliver the tetrazole, while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2. For alkenes: anodically generated azido radical adds to the alkene; the resulting β-azido C-radical is further oxidized at the anode to a β-azido carbocation, trapped by MeCN then azide to give the vicinal azidotetrazole.
rate_determining_step: Formation of the benzyl cation via stepwise electron-transfer/proton-transfer (ET/PT), as indicated by Hammett ρ = -2.97 (positive charge buildup in the transition state)

probe_reagents.name: (1-cyclopropyl-1-phenyl)ethylene (radical clock 54)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 55 isolated (21%, 7.5:1), confirming involvement of an azido radical adding to the alkene

probe_reagents.name: p-substituted ethylbenzenes (Hammett series)
probe_reagents.observation: Hammett ρ = -2.97 (r²=0.97), much more negative than typical HAT (ρ > -1), indicating positive charge buildup, inconsistent with HAT and consistent with radical-cation/ET-PT mechanism

probe_reagents.name: BDE-matched substrate 21'
probe_reagents.observation: Substrates with similar benzylic BDEs (88 vs 83.3 kcal/mol) but different electronics show distinct reactivity, disproving one-step HAT

probe_reagents.name: benzyltrimethylsilane (56)
probe_reagents.observation: Cleaves C-Si bond to afford tetrazole 7 exclusively (34%), supporting radical-cation intermediate (desilylation) rather than HAT

probe_reagents.name: TMSN3 omission control (Fig. 2G, with nBu4NHSO4)
probe_reagents.observation: In absence of TMSN3, Ritter-type acetamide product 57 forms (27%), substantiating involvement of benzyl carbocation/nitrilium intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) of ethylbenzene, TMSN3, styrene
probe_reagents.observation: TMSN3 Ep/2 = 0.92 V; ethylbenzene Ep/2 = 2.43 V (drops to 2.01 V with HFIP); styrene Ep/2 = 2.21 V — HFIP stabilizes radical cations and enables substrate oxidation; for alkenes TMSN3 is oxidized first

notes: Authors explicitly disfavor a HAT pathway for benzyl radical generation (based on Hammett ρ, BDE comparisons, and benzylsilane experiment), favoring stepwise ET/PT via the arene radical cation. For alkenes, two alternative pathways to the diazide byproduct were considered: radical-radical cross-coupling (path b, disfavored due to transient radicals) and polar trapping of carbocation II by N3- (path c, mitigated by large excess of MeCN outcompeting low-concentration TMSN3 on the anode surface). HFIP is critical as co-solvent for stabilizing radical-cation intermediates; electrolyte choice (nBu4NClO4 vs nBu4NBF4 vs nBu4NHSO4) strongly tunes selectivity between azidotetrazolation and competing azidoacetamidation.
